title: Poly-Factorial Fog: When AI, Geopolitics, and Trust Erosion Compound Simultaneously
body_html:
AI uncertainty doesn't exist in isolation. It sits atop at least three other tectonic shifts—the US-China power transition, social media's erosion of institutional trust, and AI-generated content's assault on shared reality—each of which would be destabilizing on its own. Together, they form what might be called a poly-factorial unknown: a multi-layered ambiguity where the interactions between forces are as unpredictable as the forces themselves.

The US-China dynamic is particularly potent. The United States is sliding off its pinnacle as sole global superpower while China rises in power and prestige, yet neither country knows where this trajectory leads. The psychological blow of being dethroned from a century-long position of dominance compounds domestic anxiety, while China's own breakneck ascent creates its own vertigo—moving at a thousand miles per hour with no clear destination. The uncertainty of global relationships and national identity merges with AI-driven uncertainty about individual worth and purpose.

Meanwhile, social media has replaced the managed information ecosystem of established media with something far more volatile and resistant to control. News cycles amplify uncertainty rather than contain it. Civics feels like it's headed into permanent provisionalism—a state where nothing is settled, every institution is provisional, and contrarian antics have become normalized across countries worldwide. Add AI-generated content that makes even moderate people question whether what they see, read, or hear is real, and the traditional mechanisms of trust collapse further.

What makes this combination so potent is the mutual reinforcement. AI-generated disinformation accelerates the erosion of institutional trust. Eroded trust makes it harder to build consensus on AI governance. Geopolitical rivalry incentivizes both the US and China to deploy AI aggressively while restricting cooperation on safety. Each layer of ambiguity feeds the others, creating a sustained condition that likely won't resolve in any single breakthrough moment. Leaders who treat these as separate problems to be addressed sequentially may find themselves perpetually behind.
